The mutant changed line 89 from checking "if (hoursOffset == 0 && minutesOffset == 0)" to just "if (minutesOffset == 0)". This means the condition for returning UTC is now solely based on whether minutes are zero, regardless of hours.

Now, considering fault adjacency. Faults in software can be adjacent if they're located close to each other in the code, which makes them more likely to affect similar functionalities or share common paths. In this case, the mutant changed a specific condition that affects how UTC is determined, potentially leading to incorrect time zone assignments when hours are non-zero but minutes are zero.

Looking at the test results provided, some tests started failing due to this change. For example, "TestDateTimeZone#testForOffsetHours_int" and others were broken. This indicates that changing line 89 introduced a bug because the original logic considered both hours and minutes for returning UTC, whereas now it only considers minutes.

Therefore, since the mutant directly affects the condition under which UTC is returned, any issues related to time zone offsets would be closely tied to this change. The adjacent lines in the code (lines around 86-92) are part of the same method and handle offset validation, so a fault here could have ripple effects on other parts relying on correct time zone calculations.

So, considering all this, I think the mutant's fault is adjacent because it directly impacts the core logic of determining the correct DateTimeZone instance. This change likely affects multiple test cases that rely on accurate time zone parsing and construction.
